request.userInput:
Plan my exam revision until next Friday.

I have a calculus exam next Friday morning. It covers integrals, series, and differential equations. I am weakest at series.

I have a databases exam next Wednesday afternoon. It covers normalization, SQL, transactions, and indexes. SQL is okay, transactions are weak.

I also need to submit a tiny ethics forum post by Sunday night. It should only take 45 minutes.

I can study 5 hours on weekend days and 3 hours on weekdays. I do better with spaced practice and mixed problem solving, not one huge block of reading.

Please put more time into the weaker topics while still reviewing everything.

stopReason: Accepted by 5/5 agents with no critical critiques.

planning_window.reason: Plan exam revision through next Friday when the calculus exam occurs

task_views.assessment: CRITICAL DEADLINE: Friday 2026-06-05 morning. This is the final exam in the planning window and the student identifies series as their weakest topic. With only 26 total available hours across the week, calculus must compete with databases (Wednesday) and ethics (Sunday). The calculus exam is 2 days after the databases exam, giving a narrow Thursday window for final calculus review. Series (weakest) must receive priority within calculus study. The Friday morning exam means all substantive calculus work must be completed by Thursday evening at the latest. Risk: if databases exam on Wednesday depletes energy or reveals gaps requiring remediation, calculus preparation could be compromised. The student prefers spaced practice, so series problems should be distributed across multiple days rather than crammed. Estimated 10-12 hours needed for calculus to achieve confidence across integrals, series, and differential equations with emphasis on series.

Reserve Thursday 2026-06-04 entirely for calculus final review and mixed problem solving
Integrals and differential equations review on Saturday and Sunday alongside series
Do NOT plan any calculus study on Wednesday afternoon/evening — protect energy for databases exam
Target 10-12 hours total for calculus across the week

agentViews.overall_comment: Three tasks with cascading deadlines: ethics forum Sunday night (45 min, trivial), databases exam Wednesday afternoon (urgent, 7 hours needed), calculus exam Friday morning (critical, 12 hours needed, series is weakest). Total estimated study: ~20 hours across 26 available hours. CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: Series (calculus) is the student's weakest area and requires spaced practice across multiple days — it CANNOT be crammed. This means series practice must begin TODAY (Friday 2026-05-29) and continue through Tuesday. Databases transactions must be front-loaded to Saturday/Sunday to avoid conflict with Wednesday exam. Ethics forum must be completed Sunday morning to protect afternoon study time. The Wednesday databases exam is a potential energy drain before the critical Thursday calculus review day. The plan is feasible but tight — no buffer days exist. Any illness, exhaustion, or unexpected difficulty in any topic creates deadline risk. Recommend starting series practice immediately this afternoon.

The student prefers spaced practice and mixed problem solving, which aligns with effective exam preparation. This task deserves the majority of protected study time.

agentViews.overall_comment: From a grade protection standpoint, the two exams are the clear academic priorities and deserve the vast majority of available study time. Calculus (T1) has slightly higher priority than Databases (T2) because: (1) it occurs last, giving more time for preparation, (2) the student identified series as their weakest topic with the most room for grade improvement, and (3) it covers three major topic areas requiring comprehensive coverage. Databases (T2) still deserves substantial time because it occurs earlier (Wednesday) with less buffer, covers four topics, and has a clear weak area (transactions). The ethics forum post (T3) is a minor task that must be completed but should be kept to exactly 45 minutes and scheduled during low-energy time to preserve prime study hours for the exams. Recommended time allocation: Calculus ~12 hours (46%), Databases ~8 hours (31%), Ethics ~0.75 hours (3%), leaving ~5.25 hours (20%) as buffer for mixed review, rest, and unexpected needs. This distribution protects high-value summative assessments while ensuring the ethics requirement is met efficiently.

task_views.assessment: The calculus exam requires mastering three distinct topic areas (integrals, series, differential equations) with series flagged as the weakest area. Given the student's preference for spaced practice and mixed problem solving over passive reading, this task needs significant active engagement time. The 26-hour availability window is shared with a databases exam and a forum post, so proper allocation is critical. Series alone could require 3-4 hours of intensive practice to build competency, given the student's self-reported weakness. The exam is Friday morning, so study must be complete by Thursday evening at the latest. A realistic estimate accounts for topic review, active problem practice, and buffer time for concepts that take longer to solidify than expected.
concerns: Series mastery may require more time than initially intuitive - convergence tests, power series, and Taylor series are conceptually dense topics
Active problem-solving preferred by student takes longer than passive reading but yields better retention - time estimates must reflect this learning style
Thursday has only 3 hours available and is the last full day before the calculus exam - insufficient time if series struggles aren't addressed earlier
Integration techniques and differential equations also need sufficient practice time despite series being the priority weak area
Mixing study across multiple topics (to avoid fatigue) requires more total calendar blocks rather than fewer long sessions
recommendations: Split series study into at least 2 separate blocks (e.g., 2 hours one day, 1.5 hours another) to allow spaced consolidation
Dedicate one weekend block (Saturday or Sunday) specifically to series practice with immediate feedback
Reserve Thursday afternoon/evening for a mixed review session covering all three calculus topics to simulate exam conditions
Add 2-3 hours for integration and differential equations review spread across Mon-Wed to avoid cramming
Consider allocating 9-11 hours total to calculus given the breadth and the active learning preference
suggested_subtasks: Series fundamentals: convergence tests, interval of convergence (2 hours active practice)
Series applications: Taylor and Maclaurin series, power series representations (2 hours)
Integration techniques review: substitution, parts, partial fractions (1.5 hours)
Differential equations: first and second order methods (1.5 hours)
Mixed problem sets covering all three topics (2 hours)
Past paper or mock exam under timed conditions (1 hour)
Final review and formula consolidation (1 hour)

task_views.assessment: The databases exam covers four distinct areas (normalization, SQL, transactions, indexes) with transactions identified as weak and SQL assessed as okay. The exam is Wednesday afternoon, which is the earliest deadline and reduces pre-exam study time to today (Friday), Saturday, Sunday, Monday, and Tuesday morning. This compressed timeline means transactions mastery must be prioritized early, and SQL can receive light review only. A student with active learning preferences will need to spend time working through transaction concepts (ACID properties, isolation levels, rollback mechanisms) until they click. Realistic duration accounts for conceptual understanding followed by practice problems, not just reading.
concerns: Wednesday afternoon exam leaves only 5 available study days before the exam - the window is tight for a weak topic like transactions
Transactions concepts (ACID, serializability, locking) are abstract and may require multiple passes before confidence builds
Normal forms (BCNF, 3NF) and dependency analysis require practice to apply quickly under exam pressure
SQL is 'okay' but not strong - it needs at least light review to avoid losing easy marks on basic queries
Reduced Wednesday availability (exam afternoon) means final review must happen Tuesday at the latest
recommendations: Schedule transactions study in the earliest available slot (Friday evening or Saturday morning) to allow time for the topic to settle
Split transactions into conceptual theory (1.5 hours) and applied practice problems (1.5 hours) across separate days
Normalize Sunday or Monday for normalization and indexes practice (conceptual heavy topics)
Keep SQL review brief but include at least one timed practice session to maintain speed
Allocate 8-9 hours total to databases given the compressed timeline and weak area priority

Transactions practice: concurrency problems, isolation levels, schedules (1.5 hours)
Normalization: functional dependencies, 1NF through BCNF, decomposition practice (1.5 hours)
Indexes: B-tree structure, clustered vs non-clustered, query optimization (1 hour)
SQL review: joins, subqueries, aggregations (1 hour)
Mixed past paper practice under exam conditions (2 hours)
Final review of weak areas and formula sheets (1 hour)

No calendar blocks needed for this task given the short duration - it can be completed as a single session on Sunday.

agentViews.overall_comment: Total estimated study time across all tasks is approximately 19.75 hours (calculus 10h + databases 9h + ethics 0.75h), which fits comfortably within the 26 available hours. This leaves approximately 6 hours of buffer for overflow, rest breaks, or addressing topics that take longer than expected. The main risk is the compressed timeline for databases (Wednesday exam) combined with transactions being a weak area requiring conceptual depth - this should be started immediately on Friday. Calculus has more time but series is a high-effort weak area that needs early attention. The student's preference for spaced practice and mixed problem solving should be honored by creating multiple shorter blocks rather than long marathon sessions. The ethics post is low-risk but recommend completing Sunday afternoon to preserve evening for calculus review if needed.

task_views.assessment: This is the highest-stakes cognitive challenge in the plan. The student has explicitly identified series as their weakest topic, which requires sustained mental effort to improve. The exam is Friday morning, so all intensive work must be completed by Thursday evening at the latest. From a wellbeing perspective, the key risk is cognitive overload if series is attempted in one marathon session - this topic needs to be broken into smaller, distributed practice blocks. The student prefers spaced practice and mixed problem solving, which is protective. However, I need to flag that Thursday night should remain light to allow adequate sleep before the exam - cramming series problems late Thursday would be counterproductive for both wellbeing and performance.

Use mixed problem sets that rotate between integrals, series, and differential equations to maintain engagement
Finish intensive calculus review by Thursday afternoon to allow evening for light review only
Prioritize sleep Thursday night - no late-night cramming

task_views.assessment: This is genuinely low-stakes from a wellbeing perspective - 45 minutes and due Sunday evening. The risk here is not the task itself but the framing: if the student treats this as a 'must complete perfectly' obligation alongside 5 hours of calculus study on Sunday, it adds minor but unnecessary cognitive load. I recommend treating this as a quick, low-pressure task - perhaps a short break from calculus study on Sunday evening as a cognitive palette cleanser. No wellbeing concerns with this task provided it stays contained within the 45-minute estimate.
concerns: Minimal - this task is appropriately sized

Treat as a mental break from intensive study rather than another obligation

agentViews.overall_comment: The plan is fundamentally sound from a wellbeing standpoint because the student has self-selected appropriate study hours (3-5 per day) and expressed preference for spaced, active practice rather than passive reading marathons. However, two specific risks require attention: First, series (calculus weakest topic) should never be studied in blocks longer than 2 hours - its abstract nature makes cognitively heavy stacking likely, and cognitive fatigue would reduce retention. Second, Wednesday has a natural vulnerability: the databases exam in afternoon with only 2 hours available that morning. If the student hasn't consolidated transactions understanding by Tuesday evening, Wednesday becomes high-stress. I recommend the study schedule front-loads transactions review onto Monday-Tuesday and keeps calculus series work distributed across Saturday, Sunday, and Thursday. Thursday evening must be kept very light (light review only) to protect sleep before the Friday calculus exam - this is non-negotiable for performance. The ethics forum post is a trivial addition that should not impact the schedule negatively if treated as a brief Sunday evening task. Overall workload of 26 hours over 8 days with 3-5 hour daily limits is within sustainable range, assuming no other commitments are present.

task_views.assessment: HIGH RISK: Series is the weakest area but the exam is last (Friday). The student has 6 days total but only 2 hours on Friday morning before the exam. Series requires deep understanding of convergence tests, power series, Taylor series - these are conceptually difficult topics that cannot be rushed. The assumption that 'integrals and differential equations' are stronger areas is unverified. There is no buffer if series concepts take longer than expected. The Friday morning exam means all calculus study must be done by Thursday evening, leaving zero contingency time on the actual exam day.

agentViews.overall_comment: CRITICAL CONCERN: Total available study time is 26 hours, but estimated task requirements (12 + 8 + 1 = 21 hours) leave only 5 hours of buffer across the entire week. This is dangerously thin. The databases exam on Wednesday creates a compression point - all databases preparation must finish by Tuesday evening, but calculus preparation also needs significant time before Friday. The student wants 'more time' on weak topics (series, transactions) but no explicit time allocation is specified. CRITICAL: Friday has only 2 hours available (exam in morning) - this means calculus must be COMPLETE by Thursday night, leaving zero contingency time on the actual exam day. RECOMMENDATION: The plan requires explicit time blocking for each weak topic per day, with at least 1-2 hours of contingency reserved for unexpected difficulties. Without this, the student risks being underprepared in series (calculus) and transactions (databases) despite having nominally sufficient total hours. The assumption that Friday exam is in the morning is unverified - if it's earlier than assumed, study time could be further reduced.

Both are minor because the spaced practice approach inherently provides feedback and the Friday exam day is intentionally kept free for exam preparation. The plan is acceptable with minor concerns.

dimension_scores.rationale: The calendar correctly interprets the student's exam revision request, covering both exams (calculus Friday, databases Wednesday) and the ethics forum post. Weak topics (series, transactions) are prioritized with more time allocation. The student preference for spaced practice and mixed problem solving is respected throughout. Ethics post is treated as a brief 45-minute task. The only minor gap is that calculus total study time (~10 hours) falls slightly short of specialist estimates (~12 hours), but this is acknowledged and acceptable given time constraints.

All three deadlines are protected: ethics forum Sunday morning, databases exam Wednesday afternoon with transactions front-loaded to weekend, and calculus exam Friday morning with series practice beginning immediately. Friday has zero study blocks (intentional) but this is a known weakness with no contingency. The Risk Agent flagged Friday as having no optional buffer block for last-minute review. No blocks end after their task deadlines.

The plan respects the student's stated availability (3-5 hours per day) with no overfilled days. Blocks are appropriately sized (1-2 hours typical, max 2 hours). No marathon sessions exist. The 26-hour availability is used efficiently with ~5 hours of implicit buffer for overflow or unexpected difficulties. Weekday blocks are capped at 1.5 hours maximum. Saturday uses 5 hours across three separate blocks with breaks between them.

The two summative exams receive the vast majority of study time (calculus ~35%, databases ~25%) while the low-value ethics forum post consumes only 3% (45 minutes). Series (weakest calculus topic) is front-loaded starting Friday and distributed across multiple days. Transactions (weakest databases topic) receives dedicated deep-dive on Sunday with follow-up practice Monday. Thursday is reserved for final calculus review. The plan appropriately reflects grade weight and exam relevance.

The plan is humane and sustainable. Series practice is distributed across four separate blocks rather than concentrated in one cognitively heavy session. Transactions review is front-loaded to Sunday-Monday to ensure consolidation before Wednesday exam. Thursday evening ends at 17:30 with only light series review, protecting sleep before the Friday exam. Daily workload stays within 3-5 hour limits. No late-night work or cognitively heavy stacking detected. The ethics post is treated as a brief low-pressure task.

The plan acknowledges key risks in the known_weaknesses section: Friday has zero contingency time, no buffer days exist between Wednesday and Friday exams, series concepts may require more time than estimated, and transactions understanding cannot be fully verified until the exam. The Risk Agent flagged two minor concerns: no optional Friday contingency block and no explicit self-check mechanism for understanding improvement. The thin 5-hour buffer across the entire week is a structural vulnerability. However, the front-loading strategy and spaced practice approach provide some inherent resilience.

strengths: Series practice begins immediately Friday afternoon (1.5 hours), satisfying the critical requirement to start weak topic work today
Transactions deep-dive scheduled Sunday with follow-up practice Monday, ensuring weak topic is consolidated before Wednesday exam
Ethics forum post scheduled Sunday morning (9:00-9:45), clearing the rest of Sunday for exam preparation
Spaced practice approach distributes series across four separate blocks (Friday, Saturday, Sunday, Thursday) totaling 6 hours
Thursday evening ends at 17:30 with light series review only, protecting sleep before Friday exam
Weekday blocks capped at 1.5 hours maximum, respecting daily availability limits
No marathon sessions - blocks are appropriately sized (1-2 hours typical)
All three deadlines are protected with appropriate time allocation
Student preference for mixed problem solving respected throughout
Known weaknesses are acknowledged rather than hidden, demonstrating awareness of structural vulnerabilities
weaknesses: Friday exam day has zero study blocks with no optional contingency for last-minute review if needed
Calculus total study time (~10 hours) falls slightly short of specialist estimates (~12 hours)
Only 5 hours of buffer across the entire week creates thin contingency for unexpected difficulties
No explicit self-check mechanism to verify series and transactions understanding is improving
No buffer days exist between Wednesday databases exam and Friday calculus exam - any illness creates deadline risk
The validation log shows a technical error: 2026-06-05 is marked as outside the planning window, which appears to be a boundary condition issue rather than a substantive problem
comparison_notes: This calendar was approved by all 5 specialist agents with no critical or major critiques, indicating strong specialist consensus

The planner model (minimax/minimax-m2.7) successfully balanced competing priorities: front-loading weak topics, respecting daily limits, protecting sleep before exams, and completing the low-value ethics task efficiently
The quorum requirement of 5 was met with unanimous approval, though the Risk Agent approved with minor concerns

Hard metrics show perfect scores on rejection count, critical/major counts, deadline violations, task coverage, and availability overrun, with only generation time receiving a slightly lower score (89.5)
The validation error about the planning window boundary (2026-06-05) is a technical issue that does not affect the substantive quality of the schedule
Compared to typical planner outputs, this calendar demonstrates strong adherence to student preferences (spaced practice, mixed problem solving) while maintaining academic rigor
The compromise reasoning section is detailed and transparent, explaining how conflicts between competing demands were resolved
The acknowledged_compromises sections in final critiques confirm that specialist recommendations were largely followed, with only minor deviations that are acceptable given constraints
evaluation.recommendation: Accept this calendar. It successfully balances the competing demands of two high-stakes exams with a minor ethics task, prioritizes weak topics appropriately, respects student preferences for spaced practice, and protects wellbeing by keeping Thursday evening light before the Friday exam. The unanimous specialist approval and strong hard metrics support this recommendation. The minor concerns raised by the Risk Agent (no Friday contingency block, no self-check mechanism) are acceptable given the tight timeline and inherent feedback from spaced practice. The validation error about the planning window boundary is a technical issue that does not affect schedule quality.
